Document [0] (Title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- Relapsed/Post-HSCT Acute Myeloid Leukemia) Several agents are available for patients experiencing relapsed AML with specific mutations identified through molecular sequencing techniques. Fms-like tyrosine kinase 3 (FLT3) inhibitors, such as gilteritinib, may be recommended and have demonstrated higher complete remission rates than salvage chemotherapy in this patient population. [29] Patients with IDH1 mutations, either in the relapsed setting or among older populations, unfit individuals unsuitable for induction therapy, should be offered ivosidenib or olutasidenib. [30] [31] Similarly, individuals with IDH2 mutations under the same designation may be offered enasidenib. [32] Sorfaneib is approved as maintenance therapy following allogeneic HSCT for patients with FLT3-ITD mutations. [33]
Document [1] (Title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Genes and Broad-Spectrum Drug Approvals) Other classes of drugs still have the potential to join the list of tumor-agnostic approvals. Isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) 1 and IDH2 mutations are common in cancers such as gallbladder cancer, gliomas, and acute myeloid leukemia, leading to the accumulation of 2-HG, which drives tumorigenesis. Targeted inhibitors, such as ivosidenib and enasidenib, reduce 2-HG levels, thereby restoring normal function. Vorasidenib, a newly FDA-approved IDH inhibitor, shows benefits for some low-grade gliomas in the human brain. Olutasidenib, a selective IDH1 inhibitor, has demonstrated strong remission rates in patients with relapsed or refractory acute myeloid leukemia, resulting in FDA approval. As research advances, IDH-targeted therapies continue to show promise in improving patient outcomes. [60] [ National Cancer Institute. Vorasidenib Treatment Shows Promise for Some Low-Grade Gliomas ]

These targeted therapies are used in cases where patients are refractory to initial treatment or experience relapse after standard therapy. [77]

Document [7] (Title: Leukemia Cutis -- Medical Oncology) Initial treatment of myeloid leukemia is based on age, history of prior myelodysplastic syndrome or cytotoxic chemotherapy, and performance status. The use of cytogenetics and molecular markers gained through next-generation sequencing is necessary for risk stratification and can guide upfront frontline therapy. Several molecular markers such as FLT3, NPM1, CEBPA, IDH, And other mutations are important for risk assessment. However, although karyotyping and molecular markers are robust predictors of disease-free survival, induction chemotherapy is often initiated before this information is available. Standard induction regimens have as their basis the backbone of cytarabine in combination with anthracycline popularly known as the 7+3 regimen. The therapy intends to reduce the leukemic burden and restore normal hematopoiesis. For people that are found to be FLT3 mutated, midostaurin, an FLT3 inhibitor to both ITD and TKD mutations, is now added to standard chemotherapy as part of frontline treatment based on the RATIFY Alliance Trial.

Document [16] (Title: Imatinib -- Indications) Imatinib mesylate (Imatinib) is an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor that initially received FDA approval for chronic myelogenous leukemia in 2001. Document [18] (Title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors -- Indications) Mutations, dysregulation, and overexpression of protein kinases are involved in a multitude of disease processes. Around 1 in every 40 human genes codes for a protein kinase and nearly half of those genes map to either disease loci or cancer amplicons. [1] Interest in protein kinase inhibitors began with the FDA approval of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) imatinib in 2001. Imatinib is an oral chemotherapy medication designed to target the BCR-Abl hybrid protein, a tyrosine kinase signaling protein produced in patients with Philadelphia-chromosome-positive chronic myelogenous leukemia. Since the introduction of Imatinib, the application of TKIs has been ever-expanding, particularly for cancer treatment, due to tyrosine kinases' critical roles in cellular signaling. [2] [3]

Document [23] (Title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Treatment Planning) In patients who achieve a long-term deep molecular response to therapy, discontinuation of tyrosine kinase inhibitors may be considered. Treatment-free remission can be achieved in approximately 40% to 60% of the low-risk to intermediate-risk patients. Characteristics that predict treatment-free remission include cases with low Sokal risk, no history of tyrosine kinase inhibitor resistance, and duration of tyrosine kinase inhibitor use greater than 5 years. Most relapses will occur within the first 6 months of discontinuation, and molecular monitoring should occur monthly for the first year post-discontinuation and every 6 to 12 weeks indefinitely. If molecular relapse occurs, the tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy should be re-initiated. [35]

Document [25] (Title: Pegfilgrastim -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications) Pegfilgrastim reduces the risk of febrile neutropenia in patients with non-myeloid malignancies receiving myelosuppressive chemotherapy. [4] According to the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guidelines, pegfilgrastim can be administered to prevent febrile neutropenia. [5] Pegfilgrastim is also FDA-approved to help increase the survival of patients exposed to myelosuppressive doses of radiation. Receiving acute myelosuppressive doses of radiation leads to a condition known as acute radiation syndrome, and pegfilgrastim is indicated for patients with this condition. [6]

Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021) Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.
Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326) Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.
Document [2] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.
Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.
Document [4] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

Document [7] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916) Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89) The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.

Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286) Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.
